topics.title: Array Patterns — Sliding Window, Two Pointers & Prefix Sum
topics.description: Three high-frequency array patterns that transform O(n²) brute-force solutions into O(n): Sliding Window (fixed or variable window over a contiguous subarray), Two Pointers (converging from both ends or fast/slow on same direction), and Prefix Sum (precompute cumulative sums for O(1) range queries). Kadane's Algorithm finds the maximum subarray sum in O(n) using dynamic programming on a running max.
topics.story: Imagine reading a long novel (array) looking for the most exciting 100-page chapter (subarray). Brute force: read every possible 100-page stretch (O(n²)). Sliding window: read first 100 pages, then slide one page at a time — remove the oldest, add the newest. You process each page exactly once — O(n). Two pointers: reading from both ends simultaneously to find a matching pair. Prefix sum: precompute 'total excitement up to page k' so any range query is instant subtraction.
topics.analogy: Sliding window = a peephole sliding along a fence (fixed size) or a rubber band stretching/shrinking. Two pointers = two fingers on opposite ends of a ruler moving toward each other. Prefix sum = a running odometer — to find distance between cities, just subtract two odometer readings. Kadane's = a taxi meter that resets to zero when it goes negative (dropping unprofitable customers).
topics.memoryAnchor: Sliding window fixed: r-l+1==k → slide. Variable: expand r until invalid, shrink l. Two pointer sorted array: lo=0,hi=n-1, sum<target→lo++, sum>target→hi--. Two pointer fast/slow: cycle detection, find middle. Prefix sum: pre[i]=pre[i-1]+arr[i-1]. Range [l,r] sum = pre[r+1]-pre[l]. Kadane: maxSoFar=max(num, maxSoFar+num). Max subarray = track globalMax.
topics.firstPrinciples: Sliding window works when the 'valid condition' changes monotonically as the window expands/shrinks. Two pointers work on sorted arrays or when moving one pointer closer to optimal is guaranteed by the problem structure. Prefix sum precomputes so each query is O(1) subtraction instead of O(n) scan. All three eliminate redundant recomputation of overlapping subproblems.
topics.bruteForce: Max sum of k-length subarray: for each i, sum arr[i..i+k-1] — O(nk). Two sum in sorted array: for each element, scan the rest — O(n²). Range sum query: scan [l..r] each time — O(n) per query, O(nm) for m queries.
topics.optimizedApproach: Max sum k-window: slide sum by -arr[l]+arr[r] — O(n). Two sum sorted: converge pointers — O(n). Range sum: prefix array, each query O(1) — O(n) precompute + O(1) per query. Kadane's max subarray: one pass track local and global max — O(n).
topics.whenToUse: Sliding window: subarray/substring with constraint on size or property (sum, distinct chars, etc.). Two pointers: pair/triplet problems on sorted arrays, palindrome check, remove duplicates in-place. Prefix sum: multiple range sum queries, subarray sum equals k, 2D grid sums. Kadane's: maximum/minimum subarray sum.

examples.title: Sliding Window — Fixed & Variable Size
examples.explanation: Fixed window: one operation per slide (O(1) update). Variable window: expand r until invalid, shrink l until valid again — each element enters and exits the window at most once, so O(n) total. The key insight: the window invariant (all unique / sum >= target) dictates when to expand vs shrink.
examples.realWorldUse: Rate limiting: count requests in last 60 seconds (sliding window over time). Network: max throughput in any 5-second window. Text search: find minimum window in S containing all chars of T. Monitoring: max CPU usage in last N seconds.

examples.title: Two Pointers & Prefix Sum — Kadane's Algorithm
examples.explanation: Two pointers on sorted array: converge from both ends, O(n). Prefix sum: precompute once O(n), each range query O(1) — essential for competitive programming. Kadane's: at each position, the best subarray ending here is either just the current element (restart) or extend the previous best. Track globalMax separately from running localMax.
examples.realWorldUse: Stock profit: max subarray = max profit window. Game: max score in any K-turn window. Database: rolling average over time window. Genomics: find highest-scoring gene region. Prefix sums used in 2D image processing (integral images for fast area sums).

problems.title: Maximum Average Subarray of Length K
problems.description:
Find the maximum average of a subarray of length exactly k.

**Example:**
maxAverage([1,12,-5,-6,50,3], k=4) => 12.75 (subarray [12,-5,-6,50] = 51/4)

**Key insight:** Sliding window of fixed size k. Slide by subtracting outgoing + adding incoming element.
problems.hint1: Compute sum of first k elements as initial window.

problems.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
problems.description:
Find the total number of contiguous subarrays that sum to exactly k.

**Example 1:**
subarraySum([1,1,1], k=2) => 2
subarraySum([1,2,3], k=3) => 2 ([3] and [1,2])

**Key insight:** Prefix sum + HashMap. `prefixMap[sum]` = count of times this prefix sum appeared. If `prefix-k` exists, those subarrays sum to k.
problems.hint1: Sliding window doesn't work with negative numbers — use prefix sum.
problems.hint2: For each prefix sum 'curr', count how many previous prefix sums equal 'curr - k'.
problems.hint3: HashMap stores (prefixSum → count). Init with {0:1} for subarrays starting at index 0.

problems.title: Container With Most Water
problems.description:
Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container holding the most water.

**Example 1:**
maxWater([1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]) => 49 (lines at index 1 and 8: min(8,7) * 7 = 49)
maxWater([1,1]) => 1

**Key insight:** Two pointers from both ends. Move the shorter line inward — the taller line can only decrease width, so never helps.
problems.hint2: Move the pointer with the smaller height inward (keeping larger heights is always better).
